coredata.dc:title: Carrot-derived carbon dots modified with polyethyleneimine and nile blue for ratiometric two-photon fluorescence turn-on sensing of sulfide anion in biological fluids
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Hui
Gui, Rijun
Wang, Yanfeng
Sun, Jie
coredata.dc:description: In this article, a facile and green synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) was developed by using natural carrot as new carbon source. After direct hydrothermal carbonization for 5h at 180°C, CDs were prepared facilely. Then, CDs were conjugated with polyethyleneimine (PEI) and Nile Blue (NB) chloride to produce CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites under electrostatic interactions. Upon excitation at 800nm, two-photon fluorescence (TPF) of the nanocomposites was observed, with TPF peaks of CDs at 415nm and NB at 675nm. The addition of Cu2+ could lead to TPF quenching of CDs via inner filter effect, but hardly any impacted on TPF of NB. Afterward, the added S2- combined with Cu2+ to form stable species that caused the separation of Cu2+ from CDs surface and the TPF recovery of CDs, with negligible effects on TPF of NB. Herein, a new CDs-based ratiometric TPF turn-on probe of S2- was developed and showed a good linear relationship (R 2 =0.9933) between ratiometric TPF intensity (I 415/I 675) and S2- concentration (0.1–8.0μM), with a low detection limit of 0.06μM. This probe was highly selective and sensitive toward S2- over potential interferences in real biological fluids, with high detection recoveries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Two-photon fluorescence
Carbon dots
Ratiometric
Sulfide anion
Carrot

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthetic details of CDs and CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites
Preparation of ratiometric TPF turn-on probe of S2-
Fabrication of CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites-based ratiometric TPF turn-on probe of S2-
Selectivity and sensitivity evaluation of this probe
Analytical performance of this probe in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FL spectra of CDs synthesized at 120–200°C for 5h, upon excitation at 340nm. (b) FL spectra of CDs synthesized at 180°C for 2–6h, when excited at 340nm. (c) FL spectra of CDs synthesized at 180°C for 5h, upon excitation at 310–350nm. (d) UV–vis absorption spectrum, FL (excited at 340nm) and TPF (excited at 800nm) emission spectra of CDs synthesized at 180°C for 5h.
(a) TEM image, (b) FT-IR spectrum, (c) Raman spectrum and (d) XRD pattern of the CDs synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization treatment of carrot juice at 180°C for 5h.

(a) Vis-NIR absorption spectrum, one-photon FL (excited at 450nm) and TPF (excited at 800nm) emission spectra of NB. (b) UV–vis–NIR absorption spectrum and TPF (excited at 800nm) emission spectrum of CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites. (c) Zeta potential measurements of CDs, NB, PEI and CDs-PEI-NB (CDs with PEI and NB). (d) TPF emission spectra of nanocomposites (1.0mgmL−1) excited at 800nm, after adding Cu2+ ions of different concentrations (0, 2, 4, 7 and 10μM).

(a) TPF emission spectra of CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites (1.0mgmL−1) containing 10μM of Cu2+ ions, after the addition of S2- ions of different concentrations (0, 0.1, 0.2, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 and 10μM), excited at 800nm. (b) Linear plot of I 415/I 675 versus [S2-]. (c) FL decay curves of CDs (1.0mgmL−1), CDs with addition of Cu2+(10μM), and CDs with addition of Cu2+(10μM) and S2- (8μM). (d) I 415/I 675 of nanocomposites (1.0mgmL−1) containing 10μM Cu2+ with the addition of each interference (0.1mM), the mixture of all interferences (1.4mM), 8.0μM of S2-, and “mixture + S2-”, respectively.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of CDs and CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites for ratiometric TPF turn-on sensing of S2- ions.
The determination of [S2-] in real biological fluids based on a ratiometric TPF turn-on probe of S2-.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Samples were prepared by diluting different biological fluids for 50-fold.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: All measured results were expressed as the mean of six repeated measurements ±standard deviation (SD).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Relative standard deviation (RSD) results were defined as (SD/mean) ×100%.

head.ce:title: Carrot-derived carbon dots modified with polyethyleneimine and nile blue for ratiometric two-photon fluorescence turn-on sensing of sulfide anion in biological fluids

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong Sino-Japanese Center for Collaborative Research of Carbon Nanomaterials, Laboratory of Fiber Materials and Modern Textile, The Growing Base for State Key Laboratory, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong Sino-Japanese Center for Collaborative Research of Carbon Nanomaterials, Laboratory of Fiber Materials and Modern Textile, The Growing Base for State Key Laboratory, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materia Medica, Shandong Academy of Medical Sciences, Jinan 250062, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Materia Medica, Shandong Academy of Medical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, a facile and green synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) was developed by using natural carrot as new carbon source. After direct hydrothermal carbonization for 5h at 180°C, CDs were prepared facilely. Then, CDs were conjugated with polyethyleneimine (PEI) and Nile Blue (NB) chloride to produce CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites under electrostatic interactions. Upon excitation at 800nm, two-photon fluorescence (TPF) of the nanocomposites was observed, with TPF peaks of CDs at 415nm and NB at 675nm. The addition of Cu2+ could lead to TPF quenching of CDs via inner filter effect, but hardly any impacted on TPF of NB. Afterward, the added S2- combined with Cu2+ to form stable species that caused the separation of Cu2+ from CDs surface and the TPF recovery of CDs, with negligible effects on TPF of NB. Herein, a new CDs-based ratiometric TPF turn-on probe of S2- was developed and showed a good linear relationship (R 2 =0.9933) between ratiometric TPF intensity (I 415/I 675) and S2- concentration (0.1–8.0μM), with a low detection limit of 0.06μM. This probe was highly selective and sensitive toward S2- over potential interferences in real biological fluids, with high detection recoveries.

ce:section.ce:para: In comparison with conventional semiconductor quantum dots, carbon dots (CDs) possess low toxicity, biocompatibility, low cost and chemical inertness except for having similar fluorescence properties [1]. These superior properties of CDs facilitate their promising applications especially in biomedical fields such as in vitro or in vivo fluorescence sensing and imaging [2–6]. In the past decade, CDs as a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials have attracted much attention. Similar to semiconductor quantum dots, CDs have conventional down-conversion and unique up-conversion fluorescence emissions simultaneously [7,8]. Interestingly, up-conversion fluorescence emission is an optical phenomenon wherein the emission wavelength is shorter than the according excitation wavelength, whereas conventional down-conversion fluorescence has the opposite situation [9]. Up-conversion fluorescence of CDs can be triggered by two-photon excitation (a pulsed laser with long wavelength, low energy and high penetration) [10], and thus it is also termed as two-photon fluorescence (TPF). The autofluorescence and background fluorescence from biological species (e.g. cell proteins) can be avoided with TPF, allowing a sensitive and high resolution for sensing and imaging in biomedical systems [11].

Cao et al. firstly reported the visible fluorescence of CDs when excited by 800nm femtosecond (fs) pulsed laser, suggesting the TPF properties of CDs [12]. After that, numerous researches about the TPF of CDs have been reported in succession [13–16]. Particularly, the excitation wavelength of CDs in the near-infrared (NIR, 700–1000nm) region is attractive because at a long excitation wavelength, the TPF biomedical applications of CDs are preferred because of reduced background autofluorescence, improved photon penetration and minimized photon damage to biosystems [10]. In recent years, the TPF or up-conversion fluorescence of CDs has been widely applied to cell imaging [13–21]. However, the researches about the TPF sensing of ions and molecules have been barely reported. So far, only two literatures have referred to the TPF of CDs for ions or molecules sensing. Yin et al. utilized sweet pepper as carbon source to prepare CDs through low-temperature carbonization. Based on the hypochlorite-induced up-conversion fluorescence (TPF) quenching of CDs, the CDs excited at 780nm were used as a probe of hypochlorite [8]. Zhu et al. designed a CDs-based two-photon turn-on fluorescent probe for sensing hydrogen sulfide in live cells and tissues, upon excitation at 800nm [22]. The sensing mechanisms in both literatures only depend on TPF quenching or enhancing of single TPF signal. In real detections, the exact TPF intensity is difficult to achieve due to its fluctuation at reagent concentration. However, the introduction of a second chromophore to produce ratiometric TPF dual-signal can conquer this difficulty because it is independent of reagent and chromophore contents [23–26]. To date, there is still no report about CDs-based ratiometric TPF probes. Currently, to develop CDs-based ratiometric TPF sensing is a meaningful work and is highly desired for efficient TPF sensing in biosystems.

In recent years, various methods have been exploited to prepare fluorescent CDs [1], including electrochemical oxidation, microwave irradiation, laser ablation, arc discharge methods, etc. By contrast, hydrothermal carbonization has a great advancement over previous physical methods due to its simplicity, low temperature, time-saving process and low cost, and it has been considered as an eco-friendly, facile and classical route for the preparation of CDs [27]. Recently, CDs were prepared by means of hydrothermal carbonization, using different “green” raw materials as carbon sources, such as pomelo peel [28], orange juice [29], sweet pepper [8], watermelon peel [30] and aloe [27]. Based on the point of material synthesis, the exploitation of carbon sources from readily available natural bio-resources for simple, economical and “green” synthesis of fluorescent CDs in aqueous media is an urgent requirement. In this work, a facile and green synthesis of CDs was developed by using natural carrot as new carbon source. Carrot contains several components such as carotene, protein, fat, sugar, vitamin, amino acids, etc. When these components as ample carbon sources are synchronously treated by hydrothermal carbonization, the achievement of CDs would be realized. Besides, the low cost, eco-friendly and conveniently available properties of carrot also help to its extensive use to prepare CDs.

After the hydrothermal treatment of carrot juice at 180°C for 5h, water-dispersible CDs were directly obtained and exhibited conventional down-conversion and TPF emissions. The negatively charged CDs with residual –COOH and –OH on CDs surface could conjugate with the positively charged polyethyleneimine (PEI) and Nile Blue (NB) to fabricate CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites in aqueous solution ( Scheme 1). Cu2+ ions can be captured by –NH2 of PEI to form an absorbent complex at the surface of CDs [31], which leads to the TPF quenching of CDs via an inner filter effect, but hardly any impacts on the TPF of NB [32]. Afterward, the added S2- can combine with Cu2+ to form stable species [33–35], which can cause the release of Cu2+ from PEI-Cu2+ complex and the separation of Cu2+ from CDs surface, thus resulting in TPF recovery of CDs. Meanwhile, the added S2- hardly any affects the TPF of NB.

In this article, a new type of ratiometric TPF turn-on probe was designed based on CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites. Upon NIR light excitation (e.g. 800nm), this probe could be used for efficient detection of S2- especially in biosystems. In general, S2- ions in human body can be achieved from biological metabolism or industrial processes [33–35]. The abnormal level of S2- is associated with many diseases (e.g. liver damage and Alzheimer diseases) and threatens the normal function of human body [36]. Hence, to exploit a facile strategy enabling highly effective detection of S2- in biosystems is of great importance. Compared to traditional detection technologies, e.g. inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy [37], gas chromatography-mass spectrography [38], ion chromatography [39], voltammetry [40], chemiluminescence [41], colorimetric [42], etc., the combination of fluorescence spectroscopy and fluorescent sensors to detect S2- provides a superior way for real-time, low-cost, non-invasive, highly sensitive and selective properties [43]. Among fluorescent sensors of S2-, only one article has referred to ratiometric fluorescent assay of S2- [44]. Based on the unique advantages of TPF emission, this work put forward a novel ratiometric TPF probe of S2- used for efficient detection of S2-. So far, there is still no report referring to ratiometric TPF or up-conversion fluorescence sensing of S2-.

Carrot was bought from a certain vegetable market of Qingdao, China. PEI (M.W. 1800, 99%) and NB (95%) were purchased from Aladdin. NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, NaCl, CuCl2, Na2S, acetone, ethanol and dichloromethane were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Other chemicals were of analytical grade, and all chemicals were utilized directly as received without purification. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), human serum and other biological reagents were obtained from Shanghai Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd., China. Pig urine samples were from healthy cultural adult pigs, and human urine samples were provided by healthy young volunteers. Distilled water was widely used in subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthetic details of CDs and CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of fresh and clean carrot in water. In a typical procedure, 10g of carrot was added into 40mL of distilled water, and the mixture was treated by break juicing to form aqueous suspension of carrot juice. Afterward, 20mL of carrot juice was transferred into a 50mL Teflon-lined autoclave and was heated at 120–200°C for 2–6h. After the hydrothermal treatment, the autoclaves were cooled naturally to room temperature. The resulting products were filtered with a 0.22µm membrane, followed by washing with dichloromethane to remove unreacted organic components. Finally, the resultant upper aqueous suspension containing CDs was collected, which was treated by circumrotate evaporation and precipitated with acetone. The resulting precipitates were collected by centrifugation and washed with ethanol again. After at least twice repeated post-treatments, the ultimate precipitates were collected and dried in vacuum at 60°C to obtain solid samples of CDs, or dispersed in water to form aqueous suspension of CDs for further use in subsequent experiments.

CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites were prepared based on facile electrostatic adsorption interactions. Briefly, CDs were dispersed in phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 10mM, pH7.4) to form 1.0mgmL−1 of CDs in 20mL of PBS. Under slight stirring and ultrasonication, 5mL of NB (0.1wt%) and 5mL of PEI (0.1wt%) dispersed in PBS were drop-by-drop injected into the aqueous suspension of CDs in turn, facilitating the achievement of homogeneous aqueous suspension of CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites. The following purification method of nanocomposites was similar to that of CDs mentioned above. Both the as-obtained solid and liquid samples of nanocomposites were used for subsequent experiments.

Absorption spectra were recorded with a Lambda 750S UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, US), background corrected for water contribution. One-photon fluorescence (OPF) spectra were measured by a FLSP 920 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) with a Xe lamp used for excitation at room temperature. TPF spectra were recorded on a spectrometer (HORIBA Model iHR550) and the pump laser beam came from a mode-locked Ti: sapphire laser system (Coherent Mira900). Images of transmission electron microscope (TEM) were recorded on JEM-2100 TEM (JEOL, Japan) at 200kV. Powder X-ray diffraction patterns (Siemens, Germany) were obtained by wide-angle X-ray scattering from a D5005 X-ray powder diffractometer with graphite monochromatized Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.54056Å). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra on powdered samples were recorded by a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer in transmission mode with a KBr window. Raman spectra were measured with a Horiba spectrometer using a 10× objective lens with 90s acquisition time. Zeta potential measurements were conducted with Zeta Sizer nano series laser light scattering system (Malvern Instrument Corp.). Fluorescence decay (i.e. lifetime) curves were measured by utilizing an Edinburgh FL nF900 mode single-photon counting system equipped with a H2 lamp as excitation resource.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ratiometric TPF turn-on probe of S2-
ce:section.ce:para: In 50mL of PBS (10mM, pH7.4) containing CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites (1.0mgmL−1), Cu2+ from CuCl2 was added into the PBS under slight stirring and ultrasonication. The concentration of Cu2+ ions [Cu2+] in the mixture solution (I) was adjusted to be 0, 2, 4, 7 and 10μM, respectively. As for the mixture solution (II) of nanocomposites with the addition of 10μM Cu2+ ions, S2- from Na2S was injected to form mixture solution (III), and the concentration of S2- ions [S2-] in (III) was regulated to be 0, 0.1, 0.2, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 and 10μM, respectively. Then, the TPF spectra of a series of (III) were respectively measured upon excitation with an 800nm fs laser (6.2mW). In TPF spectra of (III), the linear plotting between the increasing ratiometric TPF intensity (I 415/I 675, from CDs peaked at 415nm and NB peaked at 675nm) and the corresponding [S2-] was made. The linear plotting between I 415/I 675 and [S2-] implied the achievement of a new ratiometric TPF turn-on probe of S2-.

For the mixture solution (II) of nanocomposites with the addition of 10μM Cu2+ ions, potential interferences in biological fluids were selected and respectively added into (II), including PO4 3-, CO3 2-, Cl-, Na+, K+, Hg2+, Zn2+, histidine, cysteine, uric acid, ascorbic acid, glucose, glutathione and thiohydracrylic acid. The concentration of all interferences in (II) was fixed to be 0.1mM, while the concentration of mixture (containing all interferences) was fixed to be 1.4mM. As a comparison, S2- and “mixture + S2-” were respectively added into (II), and the concentration of S2- was fixed to be 8.0μM. By measuring the values of I 415/I 675 at different addition conditions, including each interference, S2- and “mixture + S2-”, the relative difference in I 415/I 675 could indicate the selectivity and sensitivity of this ratiometric TPF turn-on probe toward S2- ions over interferences.

To study the analytical performance of this ratiometric TPF turn-on probe of S2-, some selected biological fluids, such as fetal bovine serum (FBS), human serum, pig urine and human urine as real samples, were analyzed by utilizing this as-prepared probe. Typically, each kind of biological fluids was 50-fold diluted with PBS (10mM, pH7.4). Then, S2- ions from Na2S were added into the each diluted sample to form homogeneous mixture (50mL) with stirring, wherein the [S2-] was regulated to be 2, 8, and 14μM, respectively. After that, 50mL of mixture solution (II) containing CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites (2.0mgmL−1) and Cu2+(20μM) was added into 50mL of each real sample with different [S2-] to form probe-sample mixture solutions. For a series of probe-sample mixture solutions, the corresponding TPF spectra were measured after incubation for 5min in the dark. Based on the linear curve (in Section 2.3) between I 415/I 675 and [S2-], the [S2-] in each probe- sample mixture solution was calculated. The agreement comparison of spiked [S2-] with detected [S2-] was used for evaluating the analytical performance of this ratiometric TPF turn-on probe for the determination of S2- in real biological fluids.

In this article, to achieve superior performance of as-synthesized CDs, the temperature and time of hydrothermal carbonization reaction of carrot juice were optimized simply. After continuous reaction for 5h, the fluorescence (FL) intensity of CDs excited at 340nm gradually increases with the reaction temperature up to 180°C, but it reduces upon exceeding this temperature (in Fig. 1a). Hence, 180°C was chosen as the optimal reaction temperature of CDs, upon the identical reaction time (5h). At 180°C, FL intensity of CDs when excited at 340nm increases with the increment of reaction time, followed by a slight decrease upon the time over 5h (in Fig. 1b). According to the optimization experiments [27], we chose 180°C and 5h as the optimal reaction temperature and time, respectively. For the CDs synthesized at optimal synthesis conditions, FL properties of CDs were further studied at gradually increasing excitation wavelengths. In Fig. 1c, a red shift from 401 to 422nm was observed in the FL spectra of CDs with the increase of excitation wavelengths from 310 to 350nm, which indicates that the FL of CDs is strongly dependent on the excitation wavelength, consistent with previous literatures about fluorescent CDs [8,27,29]. This excitation dependent feature may be due to different size distribution of CDs, different emissive sites on each carbon dot and multi-photon active processes [8]. In Fig. 1c, the FL spectrum of CDs reaches the maximal FL intensity upon excitation at 340nm, indicating that 340nm of excitation wavelength should be chosen as the optimal excitation for one-photon FL emission of CDs.

In Fig. 1d, a strong UV absorption of CDs at 286nm can be mainly attributed to n-π * transition of C=O band, which is nearly consistent with CDs synthesized by carbonization of sweet pepper [8]. For fluorescent CDs, the UV absorption peak at 250–300nm represents a typical absorption of an aromatic π system [45]. Upon excitation at 800nm, the TPF spectrum of CDs is almost identical with the corresponding one-photon FL spectrum of CDs excited at 340nm (in Fig. 1d). These results suggest that the CDs can emit fluorescence peaked at 415nm, when excited both at 340nm and at 800nm, exhibiting the down- and up-conversion fluorescence emissions simultaneously [1]. The FL quantum yield of CDs was calculated to be 11.5%, using rhodamine B as a standard [22]. Using a fs fluorescence measurement technique, the two-photon absorption cross-section (σ) of CDs at 800nm was estimated to be 19.0±5.0 Goeppert-Mayer (GM) unit [32]. Generally, when compared with the one-photon FL of CDs, their TPF is more beneficial for sensing or imaging in biomedical systems. Especially upon excitation with a pulsed NIR laser (with high penetration of tissues, low photon energy and minimized damage to biosystems) [10], the as-produced TPF of CDs can avoid autofluorescence and background fluorescence from biological species, allowing a sensitive and high resolution for sensing and imaging in biomedical systems [11]. Therefore, the further exploitation of CDs’ TPF for biomedical applications is a meaningful work.

The TEM images (in Fig. 2a) of CDs reveal that the as-synthesized products are homogeneously dispersed nanoparticles with a spherical morphology and a narrow size distribution of 2–7nm in diameter. The diameters of ~81% of CDs are in the range of 4–6nm, indicating a major diameter distribution. About 35% of CDs are approximately 5nm (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). The average diameter of CDs is close to ~4.8nm, which is quite similar to that of CDs obtained from ascorbic acid [46], sweet pepper [8], and orange waste peels [47]. FT-IR spectrum was used for identification of functional groups in CDs. As shown in Fig. 2b, the CDs exhibit a characteristic absorption band of the O–H stretching vibration mode at 3387cm−1, while the C–H stretching vibration band appears at 2948cm−1. The strong absorption bands at 3387 and 1061cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups (–OH). The high-intensity bands peaked at 1612 and 1386cm−1 respectively correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylate anions (–COO-). These results reveal the presence of –OH and –COOH around surface of CDs, and thus the CDs are negatively charged in aqueous media, facilitating a facile combination of water-dispersible CDs with positively charged water-soluble PEI and NB under electrostatic adsorption interactions [8,48]. To verify the presence of aromatic carbons on CDs, Raman spectrum measurement was performed. As shown in Fig. 2c, the Raman spectrum of CDs exhibits two major bands at 1578 and 1368cm−1, which are respectively assigned to G-band (sp2) and D-band (sp3),. In general, the G band corresponds to the vibration mode of a sp2 carbon atom, while the D band represents a vibration mode of carbon atom in sp3 hybridized orbital. The presence of remarkable G and D bands suggests two types of carbon species with comparable amounts in the as-synthesized CDs. Especially the sp2 carbon atoms are considered as aromatic segments in CDs, in good agreement with earlier researches [47,49]. The crystallinity of CDs was investigated by XRD measurement. XRD patterns of CDs were shown in Fig. 2d. There is a broad amorphous peak at 2θ=22.5°, which implies the amorphous feature of CDs, near consistent with previous results relative to fluorescent CDs [8,44,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites-based ratiometric TPF turn-on probe of S2-
ce:section.ce:para: To fabricate ratiometric TPF dual-signals, water-soluble NB chloride was chosen as a reference because of its red-emitting one-photon PL and TPF. Especially upon excitation of a NIR light (e.g. 800nm), NB can emit superior red TPF peaked at ~675nm [32]. Therefore, the combination of CDs and NB helps to the formation of ratiometric TPF dual-signals. In Fig. 3a, there is a marked absorption peak located at 629nm in the absorption spectrum of NB. The one-photon PL (excited at 450nm) and TPF (excited at 800nm) spectra of NB are almost identical, showing an obvious and sharp emission peaked at 675nm. By means of a fs fluorescence measurement method, the two-photon action cross-section of NB at 800nm was estimated to be 1.5±0.5g. These results (in Fig. 1d and Fig. 3a) reveal the feasibility of fabricating CDs-NB complex for ratiometric TPF dual-signals, upon excitation at 800nm.

In this article, we prepared CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites by drop-by-drop adding PEI and NB into aqueous suspension of CDs in sequence. The resultant nanocomposites were characterized by UV–vis–NIR absorption and TPF emission spectra. As shown in Fig. 3b, there are two remarkable absorption peaks at 286 and 629nm, which are consistent with the characteristic peaks of CDs and NB, respectively. Similarly, two obvious TPF emission peaks appear located at 415 and 675nm, which are assigned to the typical TPF peaks of CDs and NB upon 800nm excitation. These results indicate the combination of CDs and NB in nanocomposites [23–26,48]. To further confirm the formation of CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites, the zeta potential values of different components were measured. In Fig. 3c, the average values of zeta potential for CDs, PEI, NB and CDs-PEI-NB were calculated to be −43.9, +16.8, +12.5 and −32.3mV, respectively. In aqueous media, reasonably the zeta potential of PEI is higher than that of NB, attributed to the adequate positively charged –NH3 + groups from PEI (in Scheme 1). Due to the presence of –OH and –COOH around surface of CDs as confirmed in Fig. 2b, the CDs are negatively charged. After the addition of positively charged PEI and NB, the zeta potential of CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites is less than that of original CDs, but the nanocomposites are still negatively charged. These results reveal that the CDs are enough to combine PEI and NB to form CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites. In other words, all added PEI and NB have been combined with CDs, and no excessive PEI and NB singly present in the aqueous suspension of nanocomposites [8,48].

In aqueous suspension of CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites, the addition of Cu2+ ions can induce a regular TPF decrease of nanocomposites when excited at 800nm. As shown in Fig. 3d, the TPF intensity reduces to near one-fourth of original intensity after adding 10μM of Cu2+. As reported, Cu2+ ions can be captured by –NH2 of PEI to generate an absorbent complex around the surface of CDs [32], which thus results in the TPF quenching of CDs via an inner filter effect. However, the added Cu2+ ions hardly any impact on the TPF of NB [35]. According to previous literatures, S2- ions can combine with Cu2+ ions to produce stable species [22,32–35,50,51]. This combination feature helps to the separation of firstly added Cu2+ ions from the substrate, after the subsequent addition of S2- ions. Therefore, S2- ions were added into the aqueous suspension of CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites containing 10μM of Cu2+ ions to study the potential effect of S2- ions on the TPF of this aqueous suspension. In Fig. 4a, upon addition of S2-, the TPF emission peaked at 415nm shows a regular enhancement, while there are negligible changes in the TPF emission peaked at 675nm. In other words, the addition of S2- induces the TPF recovery of CDs, but it only produces negligible impact on TPF of NB. Reasonably, the added S2- can trigger the release of Cu2+ from PEI-Cu2+ complex, followed by the separation of Cu2+ from CDs surface, thus resulting in the TPF recovery of CDs. The added S2- ions performing the combination with Cu2+ ions cannot interact with NB, and thus hardly any affect the TPF of NB [32].

Moreover, the ratiometric TPF peak intensities (I 415/I 675) of aqueous suspension of CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites containing Cu2+ ions gradually increase with the increasing [S2-]. As exhibited in Fig. 4b, the I 415/I 675 enhances almost linearly in the [S2-] range of 0.1–8.0μM, suitable for the requirement of detecting S2- in biological systems [22]. This plot has a good linear coefficient (R 2) of 0.9933. Based on a signal-to-noise ratio of S/N =3, the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.06μM, which is significantly lower than the recommended normal [S2-] of ~2μM in real biological samples. When compared to previously reported methods for S2- detection (Table S1), the detection range and LOD of this as-proposed method are comparable to those of other detection methods [37–43], together with a LOD approaching to the minimum. Besides, the unique advantages of ratiometric TPF further suggest the significant potential of this as-proposed method for S2- detection, especially suitable for the detection of S2- in biological fluids. The I 415/I 675 signal was nearly independent of solution pH in biologically relevant pH (5.0–9.0), implying a high TPF- stability of CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites towards pH (Fig. S2). When the nanocomposites were dispersed in PBS (at pH 7.4) at different temperatures (20–45°C, Fig. S3) or incubated at 37°C for different time intervals (1–72h, Fig. S4), the I 415/I 675 signal still maintained almost unchanged (<5%). As for the aqueous suspension of nanocomposites, these results further indicated the high photochemical stability of TPF towards incubation temperature and time.

To confirm the increase of I 415/I 675 upon addition of S2-, a further research on the TPF variation of CDs in the presence of S2- is necessary. Herein, the FL decay data were measured and shown in Fig. 4c. As for PEI-conjugated CDs, the CDs with addition of Cu2+, and CDs with addition of Cu2+ plus S2-, the according average FL lifetimes were calculated to be 7.5, 6.2 and 7.1ns, respectively. The shortened FL lifetime of CDs after addition of Cu2+ corresponds to the TPF intensity decrease (in Fig. 3d). The increased FL lifetime of CDs containing Cu2+ after addition of S2- is consistent with the TPF intensity recovery (in Fig. 4a). These results from TPF spectra and FL lifetimes further confirm that the added S2- really can trigger the release of Cu2+ from PEI-Cu2+ complex. The following separation of Cu2+ from CDs surface results in the TPF recovery of CDs. Similar results about the FL quenching and enhancement of quantum dots have been reported, and FL lifetimes in the process of adding ions or molecules were utilized to reveal the mechanism of added substances-triggered FL variation [23–26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity evaluation of this probe
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity and sensitivity of this ratiometric TPF intensity (I 415/I 675) toward S2-, the possible influences from potential interferences in biological systems were studied [26,51]. In Fig. 4d, the I 415/I 675 shows a highly selective increase over interferences, such as inorganic ions, amino acids, uric acid, ascorbic acid, glucose, thiohydracrylic acid, glutathione and their mixture. Notably, potential interferences at a coexisting concentration up to 0.1mM caused negligible (<0.03) effects on I 415/I 675. Even 1.4mM of the mixture only produced a slight change (~0.08) of I 415/I 675. By contrast, the added S2- at a concentration as low as 8.0μM could dramatically increase the I 415/I 675 (~1.36). In the presence of S2- and mixture simultaneously, this dramatic increase in I 415/I 675 (~1.39) was still obtained and was quite close to that of singly adding S2- without mixture. Although the competitive adsorption interactions of cysteine with Cu2+ enabled the formation of Cu2+-S bond and potentially caused fluorescence enhancement responses of CDs [52], the affinity or combination of Cu2+ with S2- is significantly stronger than cysteine, because of the achievement of stable CuS species [53–55]. In Fig. 4d, the presence of cysteine even at 0.1mM only caused a negligible effect (<0.05) on I 415/I 675, far less than that of S2- at 8.0μM. These results indicate that potential interferences in biological systems cannot produce marked influence even if they are present at 12.5-fold or 175-fold higher concentrations. Therefore, it is reasonable to claim that this ratiometric TPF turn-on probe really enables high selective and sensitive detection of S2-, over potential interferences in biological systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of this probe in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the feasibility of this ratiometric TPF turn-on probe for the determination of S2-, its performances were evaluated in biosystems, such as in real biological fluids (FBS, human serum, pig urine and human urine). Each kind of real fluids was only 50-fold diluted with PBS, without further purification. According to the linear relationship between I 415/I 675 and [S2-] in Fig. 4b, the detected results of [S2-] were obtained from those measured values of I 415/I 675. As summarized in Table 1, the detected values of [S2-] from this probe are in good agreement with the spiked values, with good detection recoveries (98–104%) and RSD values (1.01–3.96%). In real biological fluids, generally some electrolytes (inorganic ions), carbohydrates (small molecules), biomacromolecules (glucose) or organic components probably coexist with analytes [26,51,56]. Experimental results from detection recoveries and RSD indicate that the coexistence of inorganic ions or/and organic molecules cannot obviously impact on the detection of S2- in real samples, further testifying a high feasibility of this ratiometric TPF turn-on probe in real biological fluids.

In conclusion, a facile and green synthesis of CDs was developed by using carrot as new carbon source. The CDs were synthesized after hydrothermal carbonization for 5h at 180°C, and then conjugated with PEI and NB to form CDs/PEI/NB nanocomposites. Upon excitation at 800nm, TPF emission of nanocomposites was observed, with two apparent peaks from CDs at 415nm and NB at 675nm. The added Cu2+ led to TPF quenching of CDs, but hardly any impacted on TPF of NB. The subsequent added S2- combined with Cu2+ that triggered the TPF recovery of CDs, with negligible effects on TPF of NB. Thus, a new CDs-based ratiometric TPF turn-on probe of S2- was developed. Ratiometric TPF intensity of I 415/I 675 increased nearly linearly with increasing [S2-] in 0.1–8.0μM, with a low LOD of 0.06μM. Experimental results verified the high feasibility of this ratiometric TPF turn-on probe for the detection of S2- over potential interferences in real biological fluids, with high quantitative recoveries and low RSD.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (nos. ZR2014BQ001 and ZR2016BB37), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21305079), and the Project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (no. J14LM02).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2017.03.083.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
